topic: A student finds out their scholarship was meant for someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official letter sat on my desk, crisp and damning under the harsh fluorescent light. I traced the elegant script, the words blurring into a meaningless smear. 'Scholarship awarded to [Name of intended recipient].' My stomach tightened into a hard knot. I reread the fine print, searching for some hidden clause, some clerical error that could explain this cosmic joke. It wasn't an error; it was a deliberate, cruel misdirection. All the late nights studying, the sacrifices made—it was all for someone else. A cold, hollow feeling settled deep in my chest, making my breath shallow. I pushed the paper away, the sound sharp in the quiet room. I stood up, my legs feeling unsteady, and walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent campus quad. Every cheerful group laughing seemed like an accusation.

[story 2]

"So, this is it?" I managed, my voice sounding unnaturally flat. Professor Albright’s explanation was delivered with a practiced, overly warm smile, but the way his eyes flicked away when I mentioned the funding allocation felt like a physical slap. I clenched my jaw so tightly my temples throbbed. I kept my hands folded tightly in my lap, refusing to meet his gaze, focusing instead on a speck of dust dancing in the beam of sunlight. The reality of the situation—that the entire structure of my hard work had been built upon a falsehood—made my skin crawl. I nodded once, a tiny, jerky movement, and mumbled something about needing to collect my materials, the words tasting like ash in my mouth.

[story 3]

I kept replaying the moment the bursar announced the disbursement. The sheer audacity of it made my hands shake slightly as I gripped the edge of the table. It wasn't just disappointment; it was a deep, simmering anger that coiled low in my gut, a restless energy that wouldn't settle. I looked around the common room, at the other students who were already celebrating their own achievements, and felt a sharp, isolating distance grow between me and them. I picked up my worn textbook, the weight of the binding suddenly unbearable. I didn't need to say anything; the tightness around my mouth and the deliberate slowness of my movements spoke volumes about the unfairness of it all.
